'''Ryan Patrick Sheridan''' (born 24 May 1975) is a rugby league [[Coach (sport)|coach]] who is an assistant coach for the [[Catalans Dragons]] in the [[Super League]], and a former [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland international]] [[rugby league]] footballer. Sheridan was an [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland]] international and played at the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]].<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> Ryan Sheridan was one of the most influential players in the late 1990s for the Rhinos.

==Playing career==
===1990s===
Sheridan was born in [[Dewsbury]], [[West Yorkshire]], England, and he started his career with Dewsbury Moor ARLFC, and signed professionally with [[Sheffield Eagles]] in July 1991 making a total of 97 appearances, including 79 starts for the Sheffield Eagles in his six seasons at the club. He scored 40 tries and one drop goal.{{cn|date=October 2022}}

Having joined the [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds club]] in December 1996 from Sheffield Eagles,<ref>{{cite web |title=Rugby League: Hetherington signs three players from Eagles |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-hetherington-signs-three-players-from-eagles-1314224.html |website=The Independent |access-date=11 August 2023 |date=12 December 1996}}</ref> Sheridan was originally seen as a utility player and in his first season mixed between scrum half and hooker. However, with the arrival of [[Graham Murray]] in 1998, Sheridan came of age and was the perfect foil to the individual brilliance of [[Iestyn Harris]] with his organisational skills. He played for the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at scrum half in their [[1998 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Wigan Warriors]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Throwback Thursday: 1998 Super League Grand Final |url=https://www.totalrl.com/throwback-thursday-1998-super-league-grand-final/ |website=Total Rugby League |access-date=11 August 2023 |date=8 October 2015}}</ref>

Sheridan saved his best performances for the Rhinos for the [[Challenge Cup]], a competition he seemed to have a love affair with. In 1999 he was man of the match in all three crucial games on the way to the Final against Wigan, St Helens and then Bradford in the semi-final. His incredible pinching of the ball from Danny Peacock in the latter stages of the first half turned the game on its head and assured the Rhinos place at Wembley. Indeed, in the final but for [[Leroy Rivett]]'s late burst of four tries, Sheridan would surely have won the [[Lance Todd Trophy]]. He was magnificent under the twin towers, twice denying London in the opening quarter as they threatened to cause a huge upset. His last-ditch tackle on [[Shaun Edwards]], and then [[Karle Hammond]] steadied the ship before the second half onslaught began. Ryan rose to international status in 1999 when he earned a place in the Great Britain squad that played in the [[1999 Rugby League Tri-Nations|1999 Tri-Nations series]] Down Under. He started the first game against Australia and was then on the bench against New Zealand.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

===2000s===
In the year 2000 Ryan teamed up with St Helens' [[Tommy Martyn]] for [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland]] to form one of the most dangerous half back combinations in the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]]. Sheridan was outstanding for the Irish particularly in the crucial final group game against the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team]]. Sheridan was also back to his best in the cup again claiming man of the match awards three more times, a run which continued right up until 2002 when he again scooped the award in the memorable win over Bradford Bulls at Valley Parade. Sheridan's time at Leeds was only interrupted by a serious muscle tear in 2001 which virtually ruled him out for the whole season. In total, Sheridan made 145 starts for Leeds plus 9 substitute appearances. He scored 59 tries and four drop goals to give him a total of 240 points in the Blue and Amber. He returned to representative rugby in 2002 when he captained Yorkshire in both Origin games and then represented Great Britain against Australia in Sydney in July of that year.{{cn|date=October 2022}}

Ryan left the Rhinos in November 2002 and joined Super League rivals [[Widnes Vikings]]; the following year he joined [[Castleford Tigers]].

==Coaching career==
===Featherstone Rovers===
Ryan joined and coached alongside [[Daryl Powell]] at Featherstone Rovers. Sheridan followed Powell to Castleford in 2013, and again in 2022 when Powell became head coach at [[Warrington Wolves]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Ryan Sheridan on what makes Daryl Powell a special coach |url=https://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/sport/19907190.ryan-sheridan-makes-daryl-powell-special-coach/ |website=Warrington Guardian |access-date=11 August 2023 |date=8 February 2022}}</ref>

===Catalans Dragons===
On 29 March 2026 it was reported that he had taken the role of interim head-coach for [[Catalans Dragons]] in the [[Super League]] following the immediate and unexpected departure of [[Joel Tomkins]]<ref>{{cite web|website=Total RL|date=11 Apr 2026|author=Kasey Smith|title=Ryan Sheridan rues 'awful' Catalans Dragons start to quarter-final|url=https://www.totalrl.com/ryan-sheridan-rues-awful-catalans-dragons-start-to-quarter-final/}}</ref>

On 19 May 2026 he reverted back to the assistant coach role when [[John Cartwright (rugby league)|John Cartwright]] was announced as the new head coach, albeit on a short-term deal for the remainder of the 2026 season.<ref>{{cite web|website=Love Rugby League|date=19 May 2026|accessdate=20 May 2026|author=Ben Olawumi|title=John Cartwright lands Catalans job in shock Super League return after Hull FC axing|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/john-cartwright-catalans-appointment-19-05-2026}}</ref>
